little_template_justification: The introduction opens with a substantive puzzle about whether donors advantage moderates or extremists, but it quickly centers on conflicting findings in prior research. It positions the paper as resolving that debate with a better design and measurement strategy, so it is best read as a hybrid of puzzle and debate-resolution templates.
intro_presents_main_result_quote: Combining the RD design with my primary-specific ideology scaling, I find that the “coin-flip” primary nomination of an extremist over a more moderate opponent decreases their party’s share of general-election contributions by 7 percentage points in the median election in my sample.
intro_prior_update_quote: Combining the RD design with my primary-specific ideology scaling, I find that the “coin-flip” primary nomination of an extremist over a more moderate opponent decreases their party’s share of general-election contributions by 7 percentage points in the median election in my sample. This financial penalty increases to 18–19 percentage points when the contrast between candidates is most pronounced and is largest in highly-consequential open-seat elections.
section_titles: THEORETICAL AND EMPIRICAL PERSPECTIVES ON DONOR SUPPORT FOR MORE MODERATE AND EXTREME CANDIDATES
EFFECT OF EXTREMIST NOMINEES ON GENERAL-ELECTION CAMPAIGN CONTRIBUTIONS
WHICH DONORS PUNISH EXTREMIST PRIMARY NOMINEES?
RD ESTIMATES GENERALIZE TO UNIVERSE OF CONTESTED GENERAL ELECTIONS
STATE-LEVEL HETEROGENEITY IN THE FINANCIAL PENALTY TO EXTREMIST NOMINEES
